how: how can one method behave differently
output: object does not support property or method

1_beginner_explanation.summary: **Polymorphism** means 'many forms'. In OOP, it allows different child classes to define their own unique behavior for the exact same method name. This means you can loop through a list of different objects and call `.speak()` on all of them, and each object will respond in its own unique way, without the loop needing to know what specific type of object it is.
1_beginner_explanation.analogy: Imagine a TV remote with a 'Play' button. If you point it at a DVD player, 'Play' spins a disc. If you point it at an MP3 player, 'Play' plays audio. If you point it at a video game console, 'Play' starts the game. One button ('Play' method), many different behaviors (Polymorphism) depending on the object receiving the command.

2_internal_mechanism.execution_flow: In JS, polymorphism is achieved through Method Overriding via the Prototype Chain. If a `Dog` class defines a `speak()` method, it 'shadows' the `speak()` method defined on the `Animal` parent class. When `dog.speak()` is called, the engine finds the Dog's version first and executes it, ignoring the Parent's version.
2_internal_mechanism.memory: Method overriding creates a new function object on the Child's prototype, distinct from the Parent's prototype.

3_real_world_example.use_case: Processing a list of different shapes where each shape knows how to draw itself.

4_common_confusion.trap: Confusing Overriding (which JS supports) with Overloading (which JS does NOT support). In Java, you can have two methods with the exact same name but different arguments `draw(int x)` and `draw(String x)`. In JS, if you write the same method twice in a class, the second one just overwrites the first completely.
4_common_confusion.fix: To achieve 'Overloading' in JS, you must write a single method and manually check the arguments inside using `if (typeof arg === 'string')`.

6_edge_case.quirk: If a child class overrides a method but STILL wants to run the parent's version of that method as part of its logic, it can use the `super` keyword.
